title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
text:
Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
The Crisis of Ephemeral Environments
In computational epidemiology and public health modeling, an un-versioned environment is a non-reproducible environment. Computational outcomes depend on a complex stack of dependencies:
\[\text{Model Output} = f(\text{Code}, \text{Data}, \text{Interpreter Runtime}, \text{Transitive Dependencies}, \text{OS Kernel})\]
If any layer varies across machines, execution becomes non-deterministic. This produces the “It Works on My Machine” phenomenon, which damages the credibility of policy recommendations.
The Hierarchy of Operational Breakdown

API Drift: Upstream packages deprecate functions or alter parameter syntax (e.g., a function arguments layout changes between package versions).
Transitive Collision: Package A requires Package C at version 1.0; Package B requires Package C at version 2.0. Without an isolated lockfile engine, the global package manager resolves this via arbitrary mutation.
Floating States: Using unconstrained packages implicitly installs different builds on different dates, creating non-deterministic bug behavior across your team.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: The Core Mechanisms of Dependency Resolvers
text:
The Core Mechanisms of Dependency Resolvers
To resolve dependency trees, modern package managers convert explicit and transitive constraints into a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). They then use mathematical constraint-satisfaction algorithms to calculate a valid state across all requirements.
The Resolution Strategy

Topological Sorting: Dependency engines crawl through the graph to arrange steps so that every prerequisite library executes before the package that relies on it.
SAT Solvers: Modern package engines convert version boundaries (e.g., pandas>=2.0,<3.0) into boolean satisfiability constraints. They analyze these rules to find a single, consistent set of packages that satisfies every requirement simultaneously.
Lockfile Determinism: Once a valid graph is resolved, the tool serializes the configuration by saving exact version cryptographic hashes (SHA-256) to disk. This bypasses the search algorithm on subsequent installs, guaranteeing identical environment construction across all analyst workstations.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 2: Python Environment Determinism via uv
text:
Module 2: Python Environment Determinism via uv
Architectural Blueprint and Theory of uv
Traditional Python packaging systems (pip paired with basic virtualenv) lack built-in graph locking mechanics, leading to fragile environments. Legacy wrapper configurations like conda provide isolation but rely on slow Python-based search loops that waste valuable sprint time.
uv is an ultra-fast Python package installer and resolver written in Rust. By compiling abstract constraint logic directly to native binaries and leveraging global content-addressable caches, uv performs dependency operations 10x to 100x faster than legacy alternatives.
Step-by-Step Production Initialization
# 1. Install the native uv binary engine into your local user space
curl -LsSf [https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh](https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh) | sh

# 2. Instantiate an isolated, project-specific metadata template folder
mkdir -p ndmc_modelling_project && cd ndmc_modelling_project
uv init

# 3. Compile an isolated target execution runtime directory (.venv)
uv venv

# 4. Bind your current shell session to the isolated environment
# On Linux / macOS systems:
source .venv/bin/activate
# On Windows PowerShell environments:
.venv\Scripts\activate

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
High-Throughput Package Aggregation with uv
When you add a dependency, uv updates your tracking configuration, resolves transitive prerequisites, and updates your environment state in a single step.
# Add fundamental mathematical libraries and data visualization layers
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn

# Inject interactive prototyping engines into the isolated project loop
uv add jupyter notebook ipykernel

# Force compile the exact DAG specification into a rigid, cross-platform lockfile
uv lock

# When a collaborator pulls your code, they reconstruct your environment identically using:
uv sync






The Immutable Rule of pyproject.toml


The configuration declared inside pyproject.toml serves as the explicit blueprint for your project. Never alter this metadata manually under time pressure. Always interact with it through uv add or uv remove commands to ensure your environment remains stable.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 3: R Environment Isolation via renv
text:
Module 3: R Environment Isolation via renv
The Mechanics of R Local Library Mapping
By default, R installs libraries globally within system directories like /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/ or C:\Program Files\R\. This global design can cause system-wide issues: upgrading a library for a new project can break old data processing pipelines that depend on older behavior.
renv solves this by modifying R’s internal search path (.libPaths()). It redirects package discovery to an isolated, project-specific folder structure, preventing version leaks between different projects.
# --- Run this inside your project's RStudio console session ---

# 1. Scaffold your local directory structure and override global .libPaths()
renv::init()

# 2. Install your required data processing and geospatial health modeling tools
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("data.table")
install.packages("sf")       # For geospatial health modeling
install.packages("brms")     # For Bayesian regression modeling

# 3. Interrogate your active session memory and serialize package states to renv.lock
renv::snapshot()
Team Sync Protocol for R Users
When a new team member clones the project, they avoid package conflicts by running:
# Read the renv.lock file to download and compile the exact versions required
renv::restore()

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 4: Structural Topography (CCDS Directory Standard)
Theory of Folder Topography and Cognitive Load
In high-velocity collaborative analytics, directory layouts function as documentation. Arbitrary folder layouts force team members to spend time searching for scripts, features, and source datasets.
We enforce a strict adaptation of Cookiecutter Data Science (CCDS). This directory standard separates immutable inputs from intermediate transformations and production source files.
ndmc_modelling_project/
├── .venv/                  # Python isolated execution engine (NEVER track in version control)
├── renv/                   # R isolated project library assets (NEVER track in version control)
├── renv.lock               # Cryptographic blueprint file for R dependency states
├── pyproject.toml          # Declarative specification configuration for Python environments
├── uv.lock                 # Serialized, immutable Python graph layout lockfile
├── .gitignore              # Project gatekeeper file (prevents tracking caches and huge data payloads)
├── README.md               # Documentation: Architecture overview, setup steps, and insights
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                # Immutable source data (Read-Only; never modify these files)
│   └── processed/          # Derived feature matrices and processed data frames
├── notebooks/              # Workspace for exploratory analysis and rapid prototyping
│   ├── 01_eda_demographics.ipynb
│   └── 02_initial_plots.R
├── src/                    # Tested, production-ready modular application code
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── data_cleaner.py     # Pure data manipulation pipelines
│   └── model_engine.py     # Analytical processing and modeling logic
└── app.py                  # Final deployment file (Streamlit / Shiny app presentation portal)

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 5: Theory of Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 5: Theory of Distributed Version Control
Git as a Directed Acyclic Graph of Object States
Git does not track file deltas or changes line by line. Instead, Git models version control as a historical series of project-wide snapshots stored within a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).
Each node in this graph represents a specific state of the project, identified by a cryptographic hash. The graph grows linearly or branches out as you add new commits.
Understanding the Internals

The Blob: A compressed snapshot of a file’s content, stored independently of its filename or path.
The Tree: A directory listing object that links filenames and paths to their underlying content blobs.
The Commit: A permanent metadata record that points to a specific root Tree. It documents who created the changes, when they were made, and references the parent commit node that came before it.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
Local Git Initialization Mechanics
Before tracking code changes, configure your local environment options to ensure all commits are correctly attributed to you in the shared repository history.
# 1. Inject your identity metadata into the global configuration layer
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@ephi.gov.et"

# 2. Force the standard base branch naming protocol to default to main
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# 3. Output your current active configuration profiles to confirm settings
git config --list
Writing a Robust .gitignore Gatekeeper File
To prevent tracking multi-gigabyte data files, local environment folders, or temporary system caches, create a .gitignore file in your root folder:
# Local Virtual Environments
.venv/
bin/
include/
lib/
share/
renv/library/
renv/staging/

# Runtime Engine Caches and Intermediate Code Artifacts
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.Rhistory
.RData
.Ruserdata

# OS-Specific Hidden Metadata Files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Sensitive Input Channels (Never upload source datasets to GitHub)
data/
*.csv
*.xlsx
*.parquet
*.json

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
The High-Velocity Working-Staging-Repository Loop
To save your work in Git, you move changes through three distinct areas: the Working Directory, the Staging Area, and the Git Repository.
# 1. Review the state of your working directory files
git status

# 2. Move your tracked files into the staging area (index)
git add .gitignore pyproject.toml README.md

# 3. Create a permanent commit node in your local repository
git commit -m "chore: instantiate unified CCDS directory layout with secure gitignore"

# 4. View your project's commit history graph
git log --oneline






Notebook Sanitation Guidelines


Jupyter and R-Markdown notebooks save execution outputs directly into their underlying JSON metadata. Staging uncleaned notebooks can inflate repository sizes and cause messy diff histories. Always clear your notebook outputs before staging changes.
# Clear execution outputs before tracking notebooks in Git
jupyter nbconvert --clear-output --inplace notebooks/*.ipynb

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 6: Remote Team Collaboration via GitHub
text:
Module 6: Remote Team Collaboration via GitHub
The Decentralized Coordination Architecture
When collaborating on code, teams use an asymmetric topography: each analyst maintains an independent local copy of the repository graph on their workstation, and they synchronize their histories through a shared, central repository hosted on GitHub.
  Local Workspace [Analyst A] ───► (Feature Branch) ───┐
                                                       ▼
                                             Remote Central Engine
                                             [GitHub Core Repository]
                                                       ▲
  Local Workspace [Analyst B] ───► (Feature Branch) ───┘
Connecting Your Local Repository to GitHub
One designated team member creates a blank project organization container on GitHub and links their local repository to the remote address:
# Map your local repository to your shared GitHub organization space
git remote add origin git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git

# Verify your remote connection configurations
git remote -v

# Push your main branch to the remote repository
git push -u origin main

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
Fast Team Member Onboarding Workflow
All other team members skip local initialization. Instead, they clone the shared remote repository and set up their isolated environments instantly:

Python DevsR Analysts


# Clone the complete project repository from GitHub
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git
cd family_delta_modelling

# Reconstruct the shared virtual environment instantly using uv
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate
uv sync


# Clone the complete project repository from GitHub
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/family_delta_modelling.git
cd family_delta_modelling

# Launch an R session and rebuild the isolated package library
Rscript -e "renv::restore()"

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 7: Isolated Feature Branching
Why Modifying main Directly Ruins Team Workflows
If multiple team members push commits directly to the main branch simultaneously, the repository’s history will break down, causing frequent push rejections and broken code states.
To maintain a stable, working product, development should follow a Feature-Branch Workflow. The main branch always contains clean, working code, while all active development happens on short-lived feature branches.
# Step 1: Switch to main and pull the latest team changes
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Create and switch to a dedicated feature branch
git checkout -b feat/epidemic-sir-model

# Step 3: Implement your changes inside your modular files (e.g., src/model_engine.py)
# Model prototyping phase...

# Step 4: Stage and commit your changes locally
git add src/model_engine.py
git commit -m "feat: integrate core deterministic SIR differential equation system"

# Step 5: Push your feature branch to the remote repository
git push origin feat/epidemic-sir-model
Once pushed, go to GitHub, open a Pull Request (PR), and have a teammate review and approve your changes before merging them into main.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 8: Managing and Resolving Merge Conflicts
text:
Module 8: Managing and Resolving Merge Conflicts
Anatomy of a Conflict Node
Merge conflicts occur when two branches modify the exact same line of code in the same file, or when one developer deletes a file that another developer is actively editing. Because Git cannot automatically determine which change takes priority, it pauses the merge process so you can manually resolve the choice.
Encountering a Collision Scenario
You attempt to merge the updated main branch into your local development branch, but a teammate has already merged a conflicting change to the remote repository:
git checkout feat/epidemic-sir-model
git merge main
Auto-merging src/data_cleaner.py
CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in src/data_cleaner.py
Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

title: Computational Reproducibility, Virtual Environments, and Distributed Version Control
text:
Deciphering Git Conflict Markers
When a conflict occurs, Git writes clear structural markers directly into the affected file (src/data_cleaner.py) to highlight the differences between the competing versions:
<<<<<<< HEAD
def clean_demographics(df):
    # Your local modification written on your feature branch
    return df.dropna(subset=['age', 'gender'])
=======
def clean_demographics(df):
    # The change your teammate merged to main ahead of you
    return df.fill_na({'age': 0, 'gender': 'Unknown'})
>>>>>>> main
Step-by-Step Resolution Plan

Coordinate: Talk to your teammate to compare changes and decide on the correct logical combination.
Refine: Open the file in an editor, delete the conflict markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>), and write the clean, combined code.
Save and Push: Stage the resolved file and commit your changes to complete the merge:

git add src/data_cleaner.py
git commit -m "fix: resolve demographics data engineering merge conflict"
git push origin feat/epidemic-sir-model

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: The Problem with Manual Version Control
text:
The Problem with Manual Version Control
We’ve all been here:





Multiple nearly‑identical versions of the same document.
No clear record of who changed what and when.
Collaborating is painful – emailing files back and forth.
Risk of losing good work or mixing up changes.


Version control systems (VCS) were built to solve these problems.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: What is a Version Control System?
text:
What is a Version Control System?

A VCS tracks changes to files over time.
It allows you to:

Recover earlier versions.
See who made each change and why (with commit messages).
Work on different features simultaneously (branches).
Merge changes from multiple contributors.

Distributed vs. Centralised:

Older systems (CVS, SVN) used a central server – a single point of failure.
Git is distributed: every developer has the full history locally.



Git was created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage the Linux kernel development.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
How Git Works (The Theory)

Repository (repo): The .git folder that stores all history and metadata.
Working tree: The files you are currently editing.
Staging area (index): A temporary holding area for changes you want to commit.
Commit: A snapshot of the entire project at a point in time.

Each commit has a unique hash (e.g., f22b25e).
Commits are linked in a chain – each knows its parent.

Branch: A movable pointer to a commit. The default branch is usually main.
HEAD: A pointer to the current branch/commit.


Git Data Model

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: The Three States of a File
text:
The Three States of a File
Git files can be in one of three states:

Modified – You have changed the file but not yet staged it.
Staged – You have marked the file to be included in the next commit.
Committed – The file is safely stored in the repository.

The basic workflow: - Edit files → modified. - git add → staged. - git commit → committed.
Understanding these states is key to using Git effectively.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Setting Up Git (First‑Time Configuration)
Configure your identity (do this once per machine):
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
git config --global core.editor "nano -w"   # or your preferred editor
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
Check your settings:
git config --list --global

Use the same email as your GitHub account.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Getting Help
Git has built‑in help:
git help          # general overview
git config -h     # short help for a command
git config --help # full manual page (use Q to quit)

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: Creating a Repository and Your First Commit
text:
Creating a Repository and Your First Commit
Start a new project:
mkdir hackathon_project
cd hackathon_project
git init
Create a file and commit it:
echo "# My Hackathon Project" > README.md
git status                # see untracked file
git add README.md         # stage it
git commit -m "Initial commit with README"
git status is your best friend – use it often.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
The Modify‑Commit Cycle

Edit a file.
Stage the changes: git add <file>.
Commit with a message: git commit -m "explanation".

View the commit history:
git log --oneline
git log --oneline --graph   # shows branching structure
See what changed (before staging):
git diff
Discard unstaged changes (be careful!):
git restore <file>
Unstage a file (keep changes):
git restore --staged <file>

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Remote Repositories and GitHub

A remote is a copy of the repository hosted elsewhere (e.g., on GitHub).
You can synchronise your local repo with the remote.

Create a new empty repository on GitHub (do not add a README, .gitignore, or license – we already have one locally).
Link your local repo to the remote:
git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/repo-name.git
Push your work:
git push -u origin main
The -u sets the upstream, so later you can just use git push.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Authentication with SSH (Secure & Convenient)

SSH uses a key pair (public + private) to authenticate without passwords.
Generate a key pair (if you don’t have one):

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.email@example.com"

Copy the public key:

cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

Add it to GitHub: Settings → SSH and GPG keys → New SSH key.
Test the connection:

ssh -T git@github.com
Now use the SSH URL (e.g., git@github.com:user/repo.git) when cloning or adding remotes.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Cloning an Existing Repository
To get a copy of an existing repository (e.g., your teammate’s project):
git clone git@github.com:someuser/project.git
cd project
This automatically sets up origin and checks out the default branch.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Basic Collaboration: Pull and Push
The daily workflow when working with a team:

Before you start: git pull origin main – get the latest changes from the remote.
Make your changes, stage, and commit locally.
Share your work: git push origin main


Always pull before you push to avoid unnecessary merge conflicts.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Branching: The Foundation of Collaboration

A branch is an independent line of development.
The default branch is usually main (or master).
Use branches to:

Develop a new feature or experiment.
Isolate work from the stable code.
Keep main always deployable.


Create and switch to a new branch:
git branch feature/awesome-model   # create
git checkout feature/awesome-model # switch
# Or in one command:
git checkout -b feature/awesome-model
Push the branch to GitHub:
git push -u origin feature/awesome-model

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: Pull Requests (PRs) – The Heart of Code Review
text:
Pull Requests (PRs) – The Heart of Code Review

A PR is a request to merge changes from one branch into another (usually into main).
On GitHub, you open a PR after pushing your branch.
Team members can review, comment, and suggest changes.
Once approved, the PR is merged.

Why PRs? - Quality control – catch bugs before they hit main. - Documentation – the PR discussion explains the reasoning. - Shared ownership – everyone sees what’s changing.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Handling Merge Conflicts
Conflicts occur when two people modify the same lines of the same file.
When you git pull, Git will warn you about conflicts.
Open the file; Git marks the conflicting sections:
<<<<<<< HEAD
your changes
=======
their changes
>>>>>>> branch-name
Manually edit to keep the correct version, remove the markers, then:
git add <file>
git commit -m "Resolved conflict in <file>"

Conflicts are normal. The key is to communicate with your team.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Why Environment Management Matters (The Theory)

Every Python project depends on libraries with specific versions.
Without a lock, different machines (or even the same machine later) might get different packages, leading to “it works on my machine” problems.
A consistent environment ensures reproducibility – vital for hackathon projects where you need to share results quickly.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: Introducing uv – Fast, Modern Python Environment Manager
text:
Introducing uv – Fast, Modern Python Environment Manager

uv is a lightning‑fast package installer and resolver, written in Rust.
It replaces pip and venv with a single, unified tool.
Key benefits:

Speed: much faster than pip.
Locking: creates a uv.lock file that pins exact versions.
Project‑centric: manages dependencies declared in pyproject.toml.
Global cache: avoids re‑downloading packages.


Install uv:
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: Setting Up a Project with uv
text:
Setting Up a Project with uv
Initialise a new project:
uv init hackathon_project
cd hackathon_project
This creates: - pyproject.toml – project metadata and dependencies. - README.md. - A src/ folder (optional) for your package.
Add dependencies:
uv add pandas numpy matplotlib
Create and sync the virtual environment:
uv sync
Activate the environment:

Linux/macOS: source .venv/bin/activate
Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate


The .venv folder is created; do not commit it to Git.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
The uv.lock File

uv.lock contains the exact versions of all dependencies and sub‑dependencies.
Commit uv.lock to your repository.
When a teammate clones the repo and runs uv sync, they get the exact same environment.
This is the foundation of reproducible data science.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
CCDS Repository Style (The Why)
A well‑organised project helps everyone navigate and contribute.
Recommended structure (adapted from CCDS guidelines):
project/
├── README.md                # Overview, setup, and usage
├── .gitignore               # Ignore files (see next slide)
├── pyproject.toml           # Dependencies (uv)
├── uv.lock                  # Lock file
├── .python-version          # Python version (optional)
├── src/
│   └── project/             # Reusable Python modules
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── core.py
├── notebooks/               # Jupyter/Quarto notebooks
├── data/                    # Data (add to .gitignore if large)
├── tests/                   # Unit tests
└── scripts/                 # Utility scripts

Keep the root clean; use meaningful names.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
.gitignore – What Not to Commit
A good .gitignore prevents clutter and security leaks.
Minimal example for Python:
# Python cache
__pycache__/
*.pyc
*.pyo
*.pyd

# Virtual environments
.venv/
venv/
env/

# Jupyter notebooks (if you don't want outputs)
.ipynb_checkpoints/
*.ipynb   # or strip outputs with nbdime

# Large data files
*.csv
*.nc
*.h5
*.pkl

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp
.DS_Store

Add patterns that match your project’s files.

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
section: Challenge: Set Up a Complete Project
text:
Challenge: Set Up a Complete Project
Task: Create a new Git repository, initialise it with uv, add a dependency, and push to GitHub.

Create a directory and git init.
uv init and uv add pandas.
Create a notebooks/ folder and an empty .gitkeep (to track the folder).
Add a .gitignore with Python and uv‑specific entries.
Commit everything and push to a new GitHub repository.



Solution snippet

mkdir my_hack && cd my_hack
git init
uv init
uv add pandas
mkdir notebooks
touch notebooks/.gitkeep
echo ".venv/" > .gitignore
echo "__pycache__/" >> .gitignore
git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup with uv"

title: Version Control with Git and GitHub for Hackathon Projects
text:
Continuous Integration with GitHub Actions (Bonus)
Automate testing and linting on every push.
Create .github/workflows/ci.yml:
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install uv
        run: pip install uv
      - name: Sync dependencies
        run: uv sync
      - name: Run tests
        run: uv run pytest tests/
This ensures your code works in a fresh environment.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
text:
Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
Restoring Determinism to Statistical Modeling
In public health research, relying on global software installations breaks computational consistency. Because packages on CRAN change regularly, your statistical models depend on your specific environment:
\[\text{Statistical Output} = f(\text{Script}, \text{Data}, \text{R Core Engine}, \text{Local Package Library})\]
Upstream updates can deprecate functions or alter parameter logic, meaning that running the same script on two different machines can yield conflicting results.
Overriding R’s Search Topography
By default, R searches for libraries inside global system paths shared across all projects. To prevent package drift, reproducibility tools override R’s library discovery paths (.libPaths()), forcing the active session to find dependencies inside an isolated, project-specific workspace.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 2: Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)
Centralized vs. Distributed Paradigms
Legacy version control engines (like SVN) rely on a single centralized server. If that server goes offline or crashes, no developer can commit code, view history, or roll back broken changes.
Git operates as a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS). Every team member clones a complete copy of the repository graph, including its full historical timeline, snapshot objects, and branching metadata onto their local workstation.
Why DVCS Wins Hackathons

Offline Autonomy: Commit, branch, and view logs with zero network latency.
Redundant Backups: Every laptop acts as a full backup of the server.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 3: The Imperative Divide — Git vs. GitHub
text:
Module 3: The Imperative Divide — Git vs. GitHub
Separating the Engine from the Platform
Confusing Git with GitHub is a common pitfall. They operate at entirely different layers of your engineering workflow:








Metric
Git
GitHub




What is it?
A local open-source command-line software application.
A cloud-hosted web platform built on top of Git.


Primary Job
Tracks file snapshots, manages local branches, and computes histories.
Hosts remote code repositories, handles Pull Requests, and runs access security.


UI Environment
Terminal command line / local desktop graphics engines.
Web browsers / enterprise API integration nodes.


Storage Point
Safely contained inside a hidden .git/ folder on your laptop.
Hosted securely on remote global cloud servers.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 4: Git Workstation Installation
Setting Up Across Operating Systems
Before writing code, download and install the native Git command-line engine onto your machine:

Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)macOSWindows


Execute through your terminal package manager:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install git-all -y
git --version


Install via Homebrew or trigger the built-in developer tools stub:
# Via terminal installer stub:
git --version

# Or via Homebrew:
brew install git



Download the official Git for Windows standalone installer from git-scm.com.
Maintain standard default selections (ensure Git Bash is checked).
Open your terminal or Git Bash window and verify:

git --version

Module 5: Core Workstation Configurations
Establishing Cryptographic Authorship Identifiers
Before saving changes, establish your identity metrics globally. Git tags every single commit with this specific information to create an immutable tracking trail.
# 1. Establish your global naming identity string
git config --global user.name "Your Name"

# 2. Inject your institutional email signature
git config --global user.email "your.email@ephi.gov.et"

# 3. Enforce modern branch naming rules globally to avoid default branch syntax mismatches
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# 4. View your active configuration variables to double-check entries
git config --list

Module 6: Environment Isolation via uv
High-Velocity Python Sandbox Management
Traditional Python package commands (pip install) write libraries directly to shared system folders, which can overwrite required packages across separate projects.
uv is an ultra-fast Python package installer and resolver written in Rust. It functions as a single drop-in replacement for pip, pip-tools, and virtualenv, processing dependency updates up to 100x faster.
# 1. Install the native uv binary interface onto your system
curl -LsSf [https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh](https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh) | sh

# 2. Set up a project space and create a new environment template
mkdir -p ndmc_python_project && cd ndmc_python_project
uv init

# 3. Build a completely isolated local virtual execution environment (.venv)
uv venv

# 4. Activate the isolated layer environment
source .venv/bin/activate  # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate

High-Throughput Package Management with uv
Avoid long dependency resolution times when adding libraries right before a milestone submission.
# Install core mathematical frameworks, visualization stacks, and notebook software
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn jupyter notebook

# Force-compile your exact package graph into a rigid lockfile configuration
uv lock

# When a collaborator pulls your project changes, they mirror your setup using:
uv sync






The pyproject.toml Source of Truth


uv logs your package requirements directly inside your project’s root pyproject.toml file. Never modify this file manually under pressure; always update it through uv add or uv remove commands.

Module 7: Structural Topography (CCDS Design Layout)
Structuring Project Folders to Reduce Cognitive Load
Cluttered root directories filled with loose files like script_v1.py or data.xlsx obscure your work and break automated code tracking. We enforce a clean adaptation of the Cookiecutter Data Science (CCDS) architecture standard:
ndmc_python_project/
├── .venv/                  # Python isolated environment folder (NEVER track in version control)
├── pyproject.toml          # Declarative specification registry for Python packages
├── uv.lock                 # Serialized, immutable Python graph layout lockfile
├── .gitignore              # Project gatekeeper (ignores data files and cache metadata)
├── README.md               # Context blueprint: Explains setup steps and research findings
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                # Read-Only source files (never modify or overwrite these)
│   └── processed/          # Cleaned matrices and processed analytical data frames
├── notebooks/              # Isolated sandboxes for rapid EDA and exploratory plotting
│   └── 01_eda_demographics.ipynb
├── src/                    # Tested, production-ready modular application logic
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── data_cleaner.py     # Pure data transformation pipelines
│   └── model_engine.py     # Functional modeling execution structures
└── app.py                  # Final application layout engine (e.g., Streamlit dashboard)

section: Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
text:
Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
Mapping the Local Workspace Lifecycle
To save tracking snapshots in Git, files move across three distinct boundaries inside your local environment: the Working Directory, the Staging Area, and the Git Directory.

Working Directory: The visible folder on your machine where you actively create, modify, and delete project files.
Staging Area (Index): A middle preparation environment. It acts as a selective manifest file that collects the exact modifications you want to package into your next snapshot.
Git Directory (Repository): The permanent local archive database hosted inside your project’s hidden .git/ folder. It safely stores your structural nodes, histories, and cryptographically signed commit trees.

section: Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
text:
Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
Analyzing File Status Profiles
As you write code, every file inside your project workspace maps to one of three primary structural states: Modified, Staged, or Committed.
  [ Untracked / Edited File ]  ───►  git add  ───►  [ Staged State ]  ───►  git commit  ───►  [ Committed State ]

Modified State: You have edited code changes inside your Working Directory, but you have not logged those modifications into your Staging manifest yet.
Staged State: Your updated modifications have been cataloged within the Staging index, marking them for inclusion in your next commit milestone.
Committed State: Your packaged changes are securely saved inside your local Git database, creating a permanent, verifiable point in your repository timeline.

section: The Core Command Loop in Action
text:
The Core Command Loop in Action
# 1. Interrogate the current status of your local directory tracking state
git status

# 2. Add your structural tracking configuration files to the staging manifest
git add .gitignore pyproject.toml README.md

# 3. Permanently save your staged changes as an immutable commit node
git commit -m "chore: instantiate unified CCDS directory layout with secure gitignore"

# 4. View your local repository's history timeline
git log --oneline






Clear Notebook Outputs


Jupyter Notebook files save large execution images and output data arrays inside their internal metadata. Always clear your notebook outputs before running git add to keep repository sizes small.
# Clean notebook execution traces before staging updates
jupyter nbconvert --clear-output --inplace notebooks/*.ipynb

section: Module 10: Remote Repository Pipelines — Fork vs. Clone
text:
Module 10: Remote Repository Pipelines — Fork vs. Clone
Managing Project Permissions and Code Sharing
When you need to bring code down from an upstream repository or contribute changes to another team, use the Fork and Clone pipelines based on your access level:

Fork Pipeline: A server-side action executed through the GitHub interface. It makes an independent duplicate of an external repository inside your personal or organizational GitHub cloud dashboard. It isolates your experimental code from the primary production repository.
Clone Pipeline: A local action performed through your workstation command line. It downloads a complete copy of an existing GitHub repository onto your machine’s local drive and hooks it up to track upstream updates.

# Clone the shared team project repository onto your workstation
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/python_health_modelling.git

section: Module 11: Branching Theory — Main vs. Development Tracks
text:
Module 11: Branching Theory — Main vs. Development Tracks
Isolating Experimental Modeling Code
Never commit code directly to your production-ready tracking channels. To safely run complex modeling iterations, split your repository timeline into distinct, isolated branches.

The main Branch: Your repository’s stable production track. This branch must always contain working, error-free code that is ready for deployment or evaluation at any moment.
The development or feature Branches: Short-lived development tracks created to isolate new analytical changes, experimental algorithms, or data processing updates. This workspace keeps daily development work away from your core codebase until it is tested and approved.

Executing the Isolated Branching Workflow
# Step 1: Switch to your main branch and grab the latest updates from GitHub
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Step 2: Spin up a fresh development branch and switch to it automatically
git checkout -b feat/xgboost-pipeline

# Step 3: Write your analytical modeling code inside the modular layout
# [Active coding and sprint cycles here...]

# Step 4: Stage, commit, and push your feature modifications up to GitHub
git add src/model_engine.py
git commit -m "feat: integrate optimized gradient boosting algorithm"
git push origin feat/xgboost-pipeline
Once pushed, open a Pull Request (PR) on GitHub. Your teammates can review your additions, check for bugs, and merge the feature branch cleanly into main.

Submission Success Checklist
Before final presentation, verify:

Git repository initialised and pushed to GitHub.
Environment lock file (uv.lock or renv.lock) committed.
.gitignore excludes data/, .venv/, renv/library/, cache.
Notebook outputs cleared (or not committed).
Project structure is clean and organised.
README.md explains the project and reproduction steps.
Both main and dev branches exist and are pushed.
At least one feature branch has been merged into dev (and then into main).
Issues and project board used to track progress.
Large files (if any) are managed with Git LFS or DVC.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 5: Core Workstation Configurations
Establishing Cryptographic Authorship Identifiers
Before saving changes, establish your identity metrics globally. Git tags every single commit with this specific information to create an immutable tracking trail.
# 1. Establish your global naming identity string
git config --global user.name "Your Name"

# 2. Inject your institutional email signature
git config --global user.email "your.email@ephi.gov.et"

# 3. Enforce modern branch naming rules globally to avoid default branch syntax mismatches
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

# 4. View your active configuration variables to double-check entries
git config --list

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 6: Package State Management via renv
text:
Module 6: Package State Management via renv
Maintaining Consistent Development Environments
renv isolates your project dependencies by managing a local package library directly inside your workspace folder, ensuring your code runs identically across all team member machines.
# --- Execute this initialization script inside your project's R Console ---

# 1. Reconfigure library discovery paths and instantiate local project folders
renv::init()

# 2. Install your required data processing, spatial, and analytical modeling packages
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("data.table")
install.packages("sf")       # For geospatial health modeling
install.packages("brms")     # For Bayesian regression modeling

section: Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
text:
Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
Mapping the Local Workspace Lifecycle
To save tracking snapshots in Git, files move across three distinct boundaries inside your local environment: the Working Directory, the Staging Area, and the Git Directory.

Working Directory: The visible folder on your machine where you actively create, modify, and delete project files.
Staging Area (Index): A middle preparation environment. It acts as a selective manifest file that collects the exact modifications you want to package into your next snapshot.
Git Directory (Repository): The permanent local archive database hosted inside your project’s hidden .git/ folder. It safely stores your structural nodes, histories, and cryptographically signed commit trees.

title: Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
text:
Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
Analyzing File Status Profiles
As you write code, every file inside your project workspace maps to one of three primary structural states: Modified, Staged, or Committed.
  [ Untracked / Edited File ]  ───►  git add  ───►  [ Staged State ]  ───►  git commit  ───►  [ Committed State ]

Modified State: You have edited code changes inside your Working Directory, but you have not logged those modifications into your Staging manifest yet.
Staged State: Your updated modifications have been cataloged within the Staging index, marking them for inclusion in your next commit milestone.
Committed State: Your packaged changes are securely saved inside your local Git database, creating a permanent, verifiable point in your repository timeline.

text:
Module 10: Remote Repository Pipelines — Fork vs. Clone
Managing Project Permissions and Code Sharing
When you need to bring code down from an upstream repository or contribute changes to another team, use the Fork and Clone pipelines based on your access level:

Fork Pipeline: A server-side action executed through the GitHub interface. It makes an independent duplicate of an external repository inside your personal or organizational GitHub cloud dashboard. It isolates your experimental code from the primary production repository.
Clone Pipeline: A local action performed through your workstation command line. It downloads a complete copy of an existing GitHub repository onto your machine’s local drive and hooks it up to track upstream updates.

# Clone the shared team project repository onto your workstation
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/r_health_modelling.git

section: Module 11: Branching Theory — Main vs. Development Tracks
text:
Module 11: Branching Theory — Main vs. Development Tracks
Isolating Experimental Modeling Code
Never commit code directly to your production-ready tracking channels. To safely run complex modeling iterations, split your repository timeline into distinct, isolated branches.

The main Branch: Your repository’s stable production track. This branch must always contain working, error-free code that is ready for deployment or evaluation at any moment.
The development or feature Branches: Short-lived development tracks created to isolate new analytical changes, experimental algorithms, or data processing updates. This workspace keeps daily development work away from your core codebase until it is tested and approved.

Here are the completely rewritten, standalone .qmd slide decks for both tracks, with custom native Mermaid diagrams built into each slide.

What is Version Control?

A version control system (VCS) tracks changes to files over time.
It allows you to:

Recover older versions (like a time machine).
See who changed what and why (commit messages).
Work on multiple features simultaneously (branches).
Merge changes from different people.

Without VCS, we rely on manual backups – chaotic.


Git is the most popular modern VCS – we will use it throughout.

Git vs GitHub – The Difference







Git
GitHub




A command-line tool that runs locally on your computer
A cloud platform that hosts Git repositories


Manages commits, branches, and history
Provides a web UI, Issues, Projects, and Actions


Works entirely offline
Requires internet for collaboration




Analogy: Git is the engine; GitHub is the garage where you store and share your car.

Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)

Centralised (e.g., SVN): a single server holds all history – single point of failure.
Distributed (Git): every developer has a full copy of the repository.

Work offline, commit locally.
Sync with any other copy when online.



Key takeaway: Git is distributed – every laptop is a full backup.

section: Separating the Engine from the Platform
text:
Separating the Engine from the Platform
Confusing Git with GitHub is a common pitfall. They operate at entirely different layers of your engineering workflow:








Metric
Git
GitHub




What is it?
A local open-source command-line software application.
A cloud-hosted web platform built on top of Git.


Primary Job
Tracks file snapshots, manages local branches, and computes histories.
Hosts remote code repositories, handles Pull Requests, and runs access security.


UI Environment
Terminal command line / local desktop graphics engines.
Web browsers / enterprise API integration nodes.


Storage Point
Safely contained inside a hidden .git/ folder on your laptop.
Hosted securely on remote global cloud servers.

Centralized vs. Distributed Paradigms
Legacy version control engines (like SVN) rely on a single centralized server. If that server goes offline or crashes, no developer can commit code, view history, or roll back broken changes.
Git operates as a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS). Every team member clones a complete copy of the repository graph, including its full historical timeline, snapshot objects, and branching metadata onto their local workstation.
Why DVCS Wins Hackathons

Offline Autonomy: Commit, branch, and view logs with zero network latency.
Redundant Backups: Every laptop acts as a full backup of the server.

High-Velocity Python Sandbox Management
Traditional Python package commands (pip install) write libraries directly to shared system folders. uv is an ultra-fast Python package installer and resolver written in Rust that isolates dependencies per project directory.
# 1. Install the native uv binary interface onto your system
curl -LsSf [https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh](https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh) | sh

mkdir -p ndmc_python_project && cd ndmc_python_project

# 3. Build a completely isolated local virtual execution environment (.venv)
uv venv

# 4. Activate the isolated layer environment
source .venv/bin/activate  # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate

uv logs your package requirements directly inside your project’s root pyproject.toml file. Never modify this file manually under pressure; always update it through uv add or uv remove commands.

title: Computational Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
text:
Module 1: Theoretical Foundations of Computational Reproducibility
Restoring Determinism to Statistical Modeling
In public health research, relying on global software installations breaks computational consistency. Because packages on CRAN change regularly, your statistical models depend on your specific environment:
\[\text{Statistical Output} = f(\text{Script}, \text{Data}, \text{R Core Engine}, \text{Local Package Library})\]
Upstream updates can deprecate functions or alter parameter logic, meaning that running the same script on two different machines can yield conflicting results.
Overriding R’s Search Topography
By default, R searches for libraries inside global system paths shared across all projects. To prevent package drift, reproducibility tools override R’s library discovery paths (.libPaths()), forcing the active session to find dependencies inside an isolated, project-specific workspace.

title: Computational Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
text:
Module 2: Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)
Centralized vs. Distributed Paradigms
Legacy version control engines (like SVN) rely on a single centralized server. If that server goes offline or crashes, no developer can commit code, view history, or roll back broken changes.
Git operates as a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS). Every team member clones a complete copy of the repository graph, including its full historical timeline, snapshot objects, and branching metadata onto their local workstation.
Why DVCS Wins Hackathons

Offline Autonomy: Commit, branch, and view logs with zero network latency.
Redundant Backups: Every laptop acts as a full backup of the server.

Module 7: Structural Topography (CCDS Design Principles)
Eliminating Folder Chaos and Script Confusion
Disorganized folders degrade code readability and slow down collaborative reviews. We enforce a strict R adaptation of the Cookiecutter Data Science (CCDS) architecture standard:
ndmc_r_project/
├── renv/                   # Isolated R library configuration files (NEVER track in Git)
├── renv.lock               # Cryptographic blueprint matrix for R packages
├── .gitignore              # Project gatekeeper (ignores data assets and system caches)
├── README.md               # Context blueprint: Setup steps and model guides
├── data/
│   ├── raw/                # Read-Only source files (never modify or overwrite these)
│   └── processed/          # Derived data frames and cleaned analytical data matrices
├── notebooks/              # Exploratory scripts and initial visualization draft files
│   └── 01_initial_plots.R
├── src/                    # Standardized, production-ready modular functional code
│   ├── data_cleaner.R      # Functional data engineering and cleaning steps
│   └── model_engine.R      # Core statistical and analytical modeling pipelines
└── app.R                   # Shiny dashboard target application deployment file

title: Computational Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
text:
Module 8: The Three Areas of a Git Repository
Mapping the Local Workspace Lifecycle
To save tracking snapshots in Git, files move across three distinct boundaries inside your local environment: the Working Directory, the Staging Area, and the Git Directory.

Working Directory: The visible folder on your machine where you actively create, modify, and delete project files.
Staging Area (Index): A middle preparation environment. It acts as a selective manifest file that collects the exact modifications you want to package into your next snapshot.
Git Directory (Repository): The permanent local archive database hosted inside your project’s hidden .git/ folder. It safely stores your structural nodes, histories, and cryptographically signed commit trees.

title: Computational Reproducibility and Distributed Version Control
section: Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
text:
Module 9: The Three States of Git Tracking
Analyzing File Status Profiles
As you write code, every file inside your project workspace maps to one of three primary structural states: Modified, Staged, or Committed.
  [ Untracked / Edited File ]  ───►  git add  ───►  [ Staged State ]  ───►  git commit  ───►  [ Committed State ]

Modified State: You have edited code changes inside your Working Directory, but you have not logged those modifications into your Staging manifest yet.
Staged State: Your updated modifications have been cataloged within the Staging index, marking them for inclusion in your next commit milestone.
Committed State: Your packaged changes are securely saved inside your local Git database, creating a permanent, verifiable point in your repository timeline.

text:
The Core Command Loop in Action

# 2. Add your structural tracking configuration files to the staging manifest

# 3. Permanently save your staged changes as an immutable commit node

# 4. View your local repository's history timeline

section: Module 10: Remote Repository Pipelines — Fork vs. Clone
text:
Module 10: Remote Repository Pipelines — Fork vs. Clone
Managing Project Permissions and Code Sharing
When you need to bring code down from an upstream repository or contribute changes to another team, use the Fork and Clone pipelines based on your access level:

Fork Pipeline: A server-side action executed through the GitHub interface. It makes an independent duplicate of an external repository inside your personal or organizational GitHub cloud dashboard. It isolates your experimental code from the primary production repository.
Clone Pipeline: A local action performed through your workstation command line. It downloads a complete copy of an existing GitHub repository onto your machine’s local drive and hooks it up to track upstream updates.

# Clone the shared team project repository onto your workstation
git clone git@github.com:ndmc-davt/r_health_modelling.git

text:
Module 11: Branching Theory — Main vs. Development Tracks
Isolating Experimental Modeling Code
Never commit code directly to your production-ready tracking channels. To safely run complex modeling iterations, split your repository timeline into distinct, isolated branches.

The main Branch: Your repository’s stable production track. This branch must always contain working, error-free code that is ready for deployment or evaluation at any moment.
The development or feature Branches: Short-lived development tracks created to isolate new analytical changes, experimental algorithms, or data processing updates. This workspace keeps daily development work away from your core codebase until it is tested and approved.

section: Local Git Basics – The Three Areas
text:
Local Git Basics – The Three Areas

Working Directory – the files you see and edit.
Staging Area (Index) – a temporary holding zone for changes you plan to commit.
Repository (.git/) – the hidden folder that stores all commits and history.

Workflow: 1. Modify files in the Working Directory. 2. Stage them with git add. 3. Commit the staged snapshot with git commit.

The Three File States

Modified – changed but not yet staged.
Staged – marked to be included in the next commit.
Committed – safely stored in the repository.

Use git status to see which state each file is in.

section: Hands‑on: Create a Repository and First Commit
text:
Hands‑on: Create a Repository and First Commit
Goal: Initialise a Git repository and make your first commit.
# 1. Create a project folder
mkdir my_hackathon_project
cd my_hackathon_project

# 2. Initialise Git
git init

# 3. Create a README file
echo "# My Hackathon Project" > README.md

# 4. Stage and commit
git add README.md
git commit -m "Initial commit: add README"

# 5. View history
git log --oneline

Exercise complete – you now have a Git repository with one commit.

What is a Commit?

A commit is a snapshot of your entire project at a point in time.
Each commit has:

A unique hash (e.g., f22b25e)
Author name and email
Timestamp
A commit message (explains why the change was made)
A pointer to its parent commit(s)


git log --oneline --graph

Good commit message: short (≤50 chars), imperative, descriptive. ✅ Add data validation function ❌ changes, fix stuff

Branching – Isolate Your Work

A branch is a separate line of development.
Default branch is main.
Use branches to experiment safely.

# Create and switch to a new feature branch (e.g., for model development)
git checkout -b feature/add_model
# Work, commit, push...

Hands‑on: Branching and Merging
Goal: Create a feature branch, make a change, and merge it back to main.
# 1. Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b feature/add_greeting

# 2. Add a file (e.g., a script)
echo 'def greet(name): return f"Hello, {name}!"' > greet.py

# 3. Stage, commit, and push
git add greet.py
git commit -m "Add greet function"
git push -u origin feature/add_greeting

# 4. Switch back to main and merge
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git merge feature/add_greeting
git push origin main

Exercise complete – you now know how to branch, push, and merge.

Remote Repositories – clone vs fork

Clone – creates a local copy of a remote repository.

git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

Fork – GitHub‑only: copy a repo to your own account.

Use to contribute without write access.

Hands‑on: Clone a Repository
# Clone a public repository (example)
git clone https://github.com/ndmc-davt/example_project.git
cd example_project

# View the remote
git remote -v

Exercise complete – you now have a local copy of a remote repo.

section: Collaboration – Pull, Push, and Pull Requests
text:
Collaboration – Pull, Push, and Pull Requests

Pull – download and merge remote changes: git pull origin main
Push – upload local commits: git push origin main
Pull Request (PR) – propose merging a branch into another; enables code review.


graph LR
    A[Local Repository] -->|push| B[Remote Repository]
    B -->|pull| A
    B -->|Pull Request| C[Code Review]
    C -->|merge| B
    style A fill:#002060,color:#fff
    style B fill:#D4AF37,color:#000











Always pull before you push to avoid conflicts.

Handling Merge Conflicts

Conflict: same file, same lines, different changes.
Git marks conflict with <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>.

<<<<<<< HEAD
method = "mean"
=======
method = "median"
>>>>>>> branch-name
Resolve: edit file, remove markers, then:
git add <file>
git commit -m "Resolved conflict"

Environment Management – Python (uv)
Why: Ensure exact package versions.
Install uv:
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
Init a project:
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
uv add pandas numpy
uv lock
Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock.

Environment Management – R (renv)
Install:
install.packages("renv")
Init:
renv::init()
renv::install("dplyr")
renv::snapshot()
Commit renv.lock and renv/.gitignore.

renv creates a project-local library – no more global conflicts.

Project Structure – CCDS (Cross‑Language)
A clean, reproducible project structure works for both Python and R. The AP R template generates a slightly different structure (with analysis/, etl/, publish/, viz/), but it is equally valid. If you prefer the standard CCDS layout, use the manual alternative.
project-name/
├── checkpoints/          # Model checkpoints (if applicable)
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Original, immutable data
│   └── processed/        # Cleaned, model‑ready data
├── docs/                 # Project documentation
├── notebooks/            # Jupyter (Python) or R Markdown/Quarto
├── src/                  # Reusable functions (Python) or R/
├── scripts/              # Analysis pipelines
├── tests/                # Unit tests
├── models/               # Saved model artifacts
├── reports/              # Generated reports, figures
├── pyproject.toml        # Python (uv)
├── requirements.txt      # Python fallback
├── renv.lock             # R (renv)
├── README.md
└── .gitignore

# 5. Create placeholder files
echo "# SEIR framework" > src/seir_model.py
echo "# Parameter estimation" > src/calibration.R
touch notebooks/01_fit_model.ipynb
touch data/raw/contact_data.csv
touch reports/comparison.qmd

# 6. Stage and commit
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit: CCDS structure for measles transmission modelling"

# 7. View history
git log --oneline --graph




[Done] You have initialised a Git repository, created the CCDS folder structure, and made your first commit – all tailored to your challenge.

Viewing Changes – git diff

git diff – shows unstaged changes.
git diff --staged – shows staged changes.

# Modify your data file (e.g., survey data)
echo "Zone1, 2025, 0.12" >> data/raw/survey.csv
git diff
git add data/raw/survey.csv
git diff --staged

Use diff to review changes before committing – good practice.

section: Undoing Changes – git restore, git reset, git stash
text:
Undoing Changes – git restore, git reset, git stash

Discard unstaged changes: git restore <file>
Unstage a file: git restore --staged <file>
Undo last commit (keep changes): git reset --soft HEAD~1
Undo last commit (discard changes): git reset --hard HEAD~1
Save temporary work: git stash, later git stash pop

# Stash current work (e.g., an incomplete analysis script)
echo "WIP: incidence calculation" > src/incidence_calc.R
git stash push -m "WIP: incidence calculation"
git stash list
git stash pop

Exercise 2: Undoing and Stashing
Goal: Practice safe undo and stash in the context of your challenge.
# Create a file and commit
echo "function to load data" > src/load_data.py
git add src/load_data.py
git commit -m "Add data loading function"

# Make accidental change
echo "break" >> src/load_data.py

# Restore to last committed version
git restore src/load_data.py

# Now stash a work in progress (e.g., a new notebook)
echo "EDA notebook" > notebooks/explore.Rmd
git stash
git stash list
git stash pop

[Done] You can now safely discard or stash changes.

Exercise 3: Branching and Merging
Goal: Create a feature branch, make changes, and merge back to main.
# Ensure main is up‑to‑date
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Create a new branch
git checkout -b feature/clean_data

# Add a cleaning script
echo "clean_data <- function(df) { ... }" > src/clean.R
git add src/clean.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning function"

# Merge back to main
git checkout main
git merge feature/clean_data

[Done] You have successfully used branches.

Exercise 4: Clone and Push
Goal: Clone a repository, make a change, and push.
# Clone a public repo (replace with your own if needed)
git clone https://github.com/ndmc-davt/example_project.git
cd example_project

# Make a change
echo " - Data source: DHS 2020" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add data source note"
git push origin main

[Done] You have pushed a change to a remote repository.

Collaboration – Pull, Push, Pull Requests

git pull = fetch + merge
git push uploads local commits
Pull Request (PR) – propose changes; review before merging.


graph LR
    A[Local] -->|push| B[Remote]
    B -->|pull| A
    B -->|PR| C[Review]
    C -->|merge| B











Always pull before push to avoid conflicts.

Exercise 5: Simulate Collaboration
Pair up: One person creates a repo, the other forks/clones.

Owner creates repo on GitHub, adds Collaborator.
Collaborator clones, makes change, pushes.
Owner pulls and merges.
Switch roles.


This is the core of team Git workflow.

section: Exercise 6: Create and Resolve a Conflict
text:
Exercise 6: Create and Resolve a Conflict
Setup: Two people (or two branches) modify the same line in a script.

Branch A: changes line 1 to method = "mean".
Branch B: changes line 1 to method = "median".
Merge A into main, then try to merge B – conflict occurs.
Resolve by picking one method or combining.


[Done] You have resolved a real conflict.

Advanced: git rebase (vs merge)

rebase rewrites commit history to appear linear.
Use only on local branches before pushing.

git checkout feature
git rebase main    # move feature commits on top of main

Then fast-forward merge: git checkout main; git merge feature


Caution: Do not rebase shared branches – use merge for public history.

Tags – Marking Releases

Lightweight vs annotated tags.
git tag v1.0 (lightweight)
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Release version 1.0"
Push tags: git push origin --tags

# Create annotated tag after completing a milestone (e.g., data ingestion done)
git tag -a v0.1 -m "Data ingestion pipeline complete"
git push origin v0.1

Tags are great for marking milestones (e.g., submission deadline).

.gitignore – Ignoring Unwanted Files
Prevent committing temporary, large, or sensitive files.
# Python
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.venv/

# R
.Rproj.user
.Rhistory
.RData
renv/library/

# Jupyter
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Data
*.csv
*.nc
*.h5

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
.DS_Store

Add .gitignore early and commit it.

Large File Management – Git LFS
The Problem: Git stores every version of every file. Large files (datasets, models, images) bloat the repository, make clones slow, and can exceed GitHub’s 100 MB file limit.
The Solution: Git Large File Storage (LFS) replaces large files with small text pointers, storing the actual content on a separate server.
Installing Git LFS:
# Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt install git-lfs

# macOS
brew install git-lfs

# Windows
# Download from https://git-lfs.com
Initialise LFS in your repository:
git lfs install
Track large file types:
# Track all CSV, NetCDF, and HDF5 files
git lfs track "*.csv"
git lfs track "*.nc"
git lfs track "*.h5"

# Track model files
git lfs track "*.pkl"
git lfs track "*.joblib"

# Track large images
git lfs track "*.tif"
git lfs track "*.png"
Commit the tracking configuration:
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Track large files with Git LFS"
Push as usual:
git push origin main

Note: GitHub has a free LFS quota (1 GB storage, 1 GB bandwidth per month). For larger datasets, consider DVC.

DVC – Data Version Control (Advanced)

DVC is a tool that extends Git to handle large datasets and model files.
It stores data on cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure) or local drives, while Git tracks small metadata files.

Install DVC:
pip install dvc
Initialise DVC:
dvc init
Track data:
dvc add data/raw/dataset.csv
git add data/raw/dataset.csv.dvc
git commit -m "Add dataset with DVC"
Push data to remote storage:
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/data
dvc push

When to use: Git LFS for files up to a few GB, DVC for very large datasets (10+ GB) or when you need versioned pipelines.

Exercise 7: Large File Management
Goal: Set up Git LFS and track a large file.
# 1. Install Git LFS (if not already)
git lfs install

# 2. Create a dummy large file (e.g., 200 MB)
dd if=/dev/zero of=data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc bs=1M count=200

# 3. Track NetCDF files with LFS
git lfs track "*.nc"

# 4. Stage and commit
git add .gitattributes data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc
git commit -m "Add large climate dataset with LFS"

# 5. Push (LFS files will be uploaded separately)
git push origin main

[Done] You have successfully added a large file using Git LFS.

GitHub Issues – Tracking Tasks

Use Issues to track bugs, features, or tasks.
Each issue has a number, labels, milestones, and assignees.
Link commits with #issue-number to automatically close.

git commit -m "Fix bug #42"
git push
# When merged, issue #42 auto-closes.

Issues are essential for team coordination.

GitHub Projects – Kanban Board

Create a Project board (e.g., “Hackathon Tasks”).
Add columns: To Do, In Progress, Done.
Link issues to cards.
Visualise progress at a glance.


Great for managing hackathon workflow.

GitHub Actions – CI/CD

Automate testing, linting, deployment.
Define workflows in .github/workflows/*.yml.

Example Python test workflow:
name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest

Set up Actions to ensure code quality automatically.

Exercise 8: GitHub Issues and Projects
Goal: Create an issue and a project board.

On your GitHub repo, go to Issues → New issue.
Create an issue: “Add data validation function” (or similar for your challenge).
Go to Projects → New project (Kanban).
Add the issue to the “To Do” column.
Assign yourself and a milestone (optional).


This is how you manage tasks collaboratively.

Project Structure – CCDS (Cross-Language)
A clean, reproducible project structure is essential. The following layout is adapted from standard data science practices and works for both Python and R.
project-name/
├── checkpoints/          # Model checkpoints (if applicable)
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Original, immutable data
│   └── processed/        # Cleaned, model‑ready data
├── docs/                 # Project documentation
├── notebooks/            # Jupyter (Python) or R Markdown/Quarto
├── src/                  # Reusable functions (Python) or R/
├── scripts/              # Analysis pipelines
├── tests/                # Unit tests
├── models/               # Saved model artifacts
├── reports/              # Generated reports, figures
├── pyproject.toml        # Python (uv)
├── requirements.txt      # Python fallback
├── renv.lock             # R (renv)
├── README.md
└── .gitignore
For each challenge, adapt the naming and add specific sub‑folders as needed.

section: Exercise 9: Build a Complete Project from Scratch
text:
Exercise 9: Build a Complete Project from Scratch
Goal: Create a full project with Git, environment, and structure.
Choose your language:

Python (uv)R (renv)


# 1. Create project
mkdir my_analysis && cd my_analysis
git init

# 2. Set up Python environment
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
uv add pandas numpy matplotlib jupyter

# 3. Create folder structure
mkdir -p data/raw data/processed notebooks src
touch notebooks/.gitkeep src/__init__.py

# 4. Add .gitignore (copy the content from the slide)
nano .gitignore

# 5. Commit everything
git add .
git commit -m "Initial project setup with uv and CCDS structure"

# 6. Create remote repo on GitHub and push
git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/my_analysis.git
git push -u origin main


# 1. Create project
mkdir my_analysis && cd my_analysis
git init
Then in R:
# 2. Set up R environment
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
renv::install("dplyr", "ggplot2", "rmarkdown")
renv::snapshot()

# 3. Create folder structure
dir.create("data/raw", recursive = TRUE)
dir.create("data/processed")
dir.create("notebooks")
dir.create("R")
dir.create("scripts")

# 4. Add .gitignore (copy the content from the slide)

# 5. Commit everything
system("git add .")
system('git commit -m "Initial project setup with renv and CCDS structure"')

# 6. Create remote repo on GitHub and push
system("git remote add origin git@github.com:yourusername/my_analysis.git")
system("git push -u origin main")




[Done] You now have a reproducible project template for any hackathon.

Final Hands-On Group Activity
Scenario: You are a team of 4.

One person creates a repo and adds others as collaborators.
Everyone clones.
Each person creates a feature branch (e.g., feature/data_load, feature/clean, feature/model, feature/viz).
Work in parallel, commit, push, open PRs.
Review each other’s PRs, merge into main.
Resolve any conflicts that arise.
Final repo should have a complete analysis pipeline.


This simulates a real-world hackathon team workflow.

Collaboration – Pull and Push

git pull = fetch + merge
git push uploads local commits


Always pull before push to avoid conflicts.

Environment Management – Python (uv) and R (renv)
Ensure exact package versions for reproducibility.

Python (uv)R (renv)


Initialise a project:
cd your_project
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
Add dependencies:
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn
uv lock                # creates uv.lock – commit this!
When a teammate clones:
uv sync                # installs exact versions from uv.lock

Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock to Git.



Install renv:
install.packages("renv")
(This works the same on all operating systems.)
Initialise a project:
renv::init()   # creates renv.lock and renv/activate.R
Add a package:
renv::install("dplyr")
renv::snapshot()   # updates renv.lock
When a teammate clones:
renv::restore()    # installs packages from renv.lock

Commit renv.lock and renv/.gitignore to Git.

Four Families – Final Team Project
Divide into four groups (families). Each family will build a project from scratch, following the main and dev branch model.
Project names and topics:








Family
Project Name
Problem / Focus




Alpha
malaria-incidence
Analyse malaria incidence trends across Ethiopian zones using historical data.


Beta
climate-extremes
Model climate extremes (temperature, precipitation) and their impact on agriculture.


Gamma
health-facility-mapping
Map health facility distribution and accessibility in urban/rural areas.


Delta
crop-yield-prediction
Predict crop yields using satellite imagery and weather data.



Instructions for each family:

Create the repository on GitHub (one person per family) – add all members as collaborators.
Clone the repo to each member’s machine.
Create a dev branch from main:
git checkout -b dev
git push -u origin dev
Each member creates a feature branch from dev (e.g., feature/data-load, feature/clean, feature/model, feature/viz).
Work in parallel – each member:

Writes code for their assigned task.
Stages and commits with clear messages.
Pushes their branch to GitHub.

Merge features into dev via local merges (or use Pull Requests if you have time).
Resolve any conflicts that arise.
When all features are integrated into dev, merge dev into main.
Final check:

Repository has main and dev branches.
All code is in the correct CCDS folder structure.
Environment lock file (uv.lock or renv.lock) is committed.
.gitignore excludes data and cache.
README.md describes the project and how to reproduce.



This exercise simulates a real hackathon team workflow with a clear branch strategy.

Team Challenges – Final Project (Four Groups)
Divide into four teams. Each team tackles one challenge using the main/dev branch model.



Family
Challenge

When ready, merge dev into main. 8. section: Installing uv – Python Environment Manager (All OS)
text:
Installing uv – Python Environment Manager (All OS)
uv is a fast Python package installer and resolver. Install it before using it in your projects.

Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)macOSWindows


curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

wget -qO- https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

brew install uv

powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"

After installation, verify with uv --version. The uv command should be available in your terminal.

Automating Project Setup with Cookiecutter
Cookiecutter is a command-line tool that generates projects from templates. It saves time and enforces consistency across teams.
Installing and Using Cookiecutter

PythonR


Install cookiecutter:
pip install cookiecutter
Generate a data science project:

You will be prompted for: - project_name: e.g., zero-dose-burden - repo_name: (defaults to project_name) - author_name: Your name - description: Brief project description - Other optional fields

git init
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit from Cookiecutter template"

install.packages("cookiecutter")
Then in R:

  "https://github.com/drivendata/cookiecutter-data-science",
  # Or use an R‑specific template:
  # "https://github.com/datadesk/cookiecutter-r-project"
)
Option 2 – Use usethis with RStudio project templates:

Then manually add sub‑folders:
dir.create("data/raw", recursive = TRUE)
dir.create("data/processed")
dir.create("notebooks")
dir.create("R")
dir.create("reports")

# Use an R‑focused template

This template sets up: - R/ for functions - data/ for raw/processed data - reports/ for R Markdown outputs - README.md, LICENSE, and a basic project structure.




Tip: Cookiecutter is a one‑time setup. After generating the structure, you continue using Git and your environment manager (uv or renv) as usual.

section: Environment Management – Using uv (Python) and renv (R)
text:
Environment Management – Using uv (Python) and renv (R)
After installing uv (earlier), here is how to use it in your project.

Python (uv)R (renv)


Initialise a project:
cd your_project
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
Add dependencies:
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn
uv lock                # creates uv.lock – commit this!
When a teammate clones:
uv sync                # installs exact versions from uv.lock

Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock to Git.



Install renv (once per machine):
install.packages("renv")
Initialise a project:
renv::init()   # creates renv.lock and renv/activate.R
Add a package:
renv::install("dplyr")
renv::snapshot()   # updates renv.lock
When a teammate clones:
renv::restore()    # installs packages from renv.lock

Commit renv.lock and renv/.gitignore to Git.

Command

Command
