section: Exercise: Create and Resolve a Conflict
text:
Exercise: Create and Resolve a Conflict
Setup: Two people (or two branches) modify the same line in a script.

Branch A: changes line 1 to method = "mean".
Branch B: changes line 1 to method = "median".
Merge A into main, then try to merge B – conflict occurs.
Resolve by picking one method or combining.


[Done] You have resolved a real conflict.

Exercise: Large File Management
Goal: Set up Git LFS and track a large file.
# 1. Install Git LFS (if not already)
git lfs install

# 2. Create a dummy large file (e.g., 200 MB) simulating a climate dataset
dd if=/dev/zero of=data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc bs=1M count=200

# 3. Track NetCDF files with LFS
git lfs track "*.nc"

# 4. Stage and commit
git add .gitattributes data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc
git commit -m "Add large climate dataset with LFS"

# 5. Push (LFS files will be uploaded separately)
git push origin main

[Done] You have successfully added a large file using Git LFS.

Exercise: GitHub Issues and Projects
Goal: Create an issue and a project board.

On your GitHub repo, go to Issues → New issue.
Create an issue: “Add data validation function” (or similar for your challenge).
Go to Projects → New project (Kanban).
Add the issue to the “To Do” column.
Assign yourself and a milestone (optional).


This is how you manage tasks collaboratively.

Git Tips and Tricks

Commit early, commit often – small, frequent commits are easier to review and revert if needed.
Write meaningful commit messages – use imperative mood, keep under 50 characters, explain why not how.
Run git status before and after every command – it prevents surprises and keeps you oriented.
Always git pull before you git push – this avoids unnecessary merge conflicts.
Use branches for every new feature or experiment – keep main stable; branch names like feature/description help.
Stash unfinished work – use git stash push -m "WIP" to save work in progress, then git stash pop to restore.
Review changes with git diff before staging – catch mistakes before they become commits.
Use git log --oneline --graph for a clear history view – visualise branches and merges at a glance.
Add .gitignore at the start – prevent accidental commits of data files, credentials, or cache folders.
Use Git LFS or DVC for large files – avoid repository bloat and stay within GitHub’s size limits.
If in doubt, git help or git <command> --help – the built‑in documentation is excellent.

Branching Architecture
Coordinating Parallel Workflows with Main, Dev, and Subbranches
Never develop directly on production timelines. We isolate our codebase into an ecosystem of stable execution tracks and rapid-iteration subbranches:

main: The pristine production baseline. Contains fully verified, peer-reviewed, deployment-ready code releases.
dev: The ongoing compilation line. Merges stable internal features before prepping a public version bump.
feat/*: Experimental subbranches. Isolated environments spun out from dev to prototype single components (e.g., analytical modules) without breaking the team baseline.

Integrating via Git Merge
Context Preservation and the Non-Destructive Timeline
When a development feature is complete and peer-reviewed, git merge brings those changes back into the parent track.

Mechanics: Git creates a specialized, dedicated Merge Commit node that binds the history of both paths together.
Value: It provides an absolute audit log. The repository graph records exactly when an experimental subbranch was built and when it was joined back into dev.
Command Loop: bash   git checkout dev   git merge feat-data-cleaner --no-ff -m "merge: incorporate completed data pipeline"

Streamlining via Git Rebase
Rewriting Commit Topography for Pristine Linear Histories
As teams scale, multiple intersecting merge loops can clutter the project timeline. git rebase solves this by picking up your local commits and re-applying them directly on top of the newest parent branch updates.

Mechanics: It temporarily side-lines your feature updates, synchronizes your subbranch baseline with the current state of dev, and writes your modifications linearly on top.
Value: Eliminates messy intersection lines. The final path appears as a completely straightforward timeline.
Command Loop: bash   git checkout feat-spatial-model   git rebase dev

What is Version Control?

A version control system (VCS) tracks changes to files over time.
It allows you to:

Recover older versions (like a time machine).
See who changed what and why (commit messages).
Work on multiple features simultaneously (branches).
Merge changes from different people.

Without VCS, we rely on manual backups – chaotic.


Git is the most popular modern VCS – we will use it throughout.

Git vs GitHub – The Difference







Git
GitHub




A command-line tool that runs locally on your computer
A cloud platform that hosts Git repositories


Manages commits, branches, and history
Provides a web UI, Issues, Projects, and Actions


Works entirely offline
Requires internet for collaboration




Analogy: Git is the engine; GitHub is the garage where you store and share your car.

Distributed Version Control Systems (DVCS)

Centralised (e.g., SVN): a single server holds all history – single point of failure.
Distributed (Git): every developer has a full copy of the repository.

Work offline, commit locally.
Sync with any other copy when online.



Key takeaway: Git is distributed – every laptop is a full backup.

# or use the built-in xcode-select stub

section: Local Git Basics – The Three Areas
text:
Local Git Basics – The Three Areas

Working Directory – the files you see and edit.
Staging Area (Index) – a temporary holding zone for changes you plan to commit.
Repository (.git/) – the hidden folder that stores all commits and history.

Workflow: 1. Modify files in the Working Directory. 2. Stage them with git add. 3. Commit the staged snapshot with git commit.

The Three File States

Modified – changed but not yet staged.
Staged – marked to be included in the next commit.
Committed – safely stored in the repository.

Use git status to see which state each file is in.

Viewing Changes – git diff

git diff – shows unstaged changes.
git diff --staged – shows staged changes.

section: Undoing Changes – git restore, git reset, git stash
text:
Undoing Changes – git restore, git reset, git stash

Discard unstaged changes: git restore <file>
Unstage a file: git restore --staged <file>
Undo last commit (keep changes): git reset --soft HEAD~1
Undo last commit (discard changes): git reset --hard HEAD~1
Save temporary work: git stash, later git stash pop

# Stash current work (e.g., an incomplete analysis script)
echo "WIP: incidence calculation" > src/incidence_calc.R
git stash push -m "WIP: incidence calculation"
git stash list
git stash pop

Exercise: Practice Undoing and Stashing
Goal: Safely discard changes and stash work in progress.
# Create and commit a data loading function
echo "load_data <- function() {}" > src/load_data.R
git add src/load_data.R
git commit -m "Add data loading function"

# Make accidental change
echo "break" >> src/load_data.R
git restore src/load_data.R   # restore

# Stash a new exploratory notebook
echo "EDA for zero-dose" > notebooks/explore.Rmd
git stash
git stash list
git stash pop

[Done] You can now safely undo changes and stash work.

Branching – Isolate Your Work

A branch is a separate line of development.
Default branch is main.
Use branches to experiment safely.

section: Branching Strategy – main, dev, and feature branches
text:
Branching Strategy – main, dev, and feature branches








Branch
Purpose
Who creates it




main
Production‑ready, stable code. Only merge from dev after testing.
Created by git init (default).


dev
Integration branch. All feature branches merge here first.
Created manually from main.


feature/*
Individual features or experiments. Branch from dev, merge back to dev.
Each developer creates their own.



main  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────►
   │
   └── dev  ──────────────────────────────────────────►
          │       │       │
          ├── feature/clean_data ───┘
          ├── feature/model ────────┘
          └── feature/viz ──────────┘

Why this matters: main stays clean and deployable. dev is where integration happens. Feature branches are short‑lived and isolated.

Creating the dev branch
After initializing your repository, create the dev branch:
# Switch to main (ensure you are there)
git switch main

# Create dev branch from main
git switch -c dev

# Push dev to GitHub and set upstream
git push -u origin dev
Now your repository has two permanent branches: main and dev.

Feature branches from dev
When starting a new task, always branch from dev:
# Ensure you are on dev with the latest changes
git switch dev
git pull origin dev

# Create a feature branch
git switch -c feature/clean_data

# Work, commit, push...
git add src/clean.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning function"
git push -u origin feature/clean_data

Merging feature branches into dev
Once a feature is complete, merge it back into dev:
# Switch to dev and pull latest
git switch dev
git pull origin dev

# Merge the feature branch
git merge feature/clean_data

# Push updated dev
git push origin dev

Merging dev into main (release)
When all features in dev are stable and tested, merge into main:
# Switch to main and pull latest
git switch main
git pull origin main

# Merge dev into main
git merge dev

# Push updated main
git push origin main

Tip: Use git merge --no-ff dev to keep a visible merge commit.

Exercise: Branching and Merging (main/dev strategy)
Goal: Create a feature branch from dev, make changes, merge back to dev, then merge dev into main.
# 1. Ensure dev is up‑to‑date
git switch dev
git pull origin dev

# 2. Create a feature branch for data cleaning
git switch -c feature/clean_data

# 3. Add and commit your changes
echo "clean_survey <- function(df) { ... }" > src/clean.R
git add src/clean.R
git commit -m "Add data cleaning function"

# 4. Push feature branch
git push -u origin feature/clean_data

# 5. Merge feature into dev
git switch dev
git merge feature/clean_data
git push origin dev

# 6. Merge dev into main (when ready)
git switch main
git merge dev
git push origin main

[Done] You have successfully used the main/dev/feature branch strategy.

Git Branching and History Commands








Command
Description
Example (challenge‑relevant)




git branch
List all branches
git branch


git branch <name>
Create a new branch
git branch feature/clean_data


git switch <branch>
Switch to an existing branch (newer, safer)
git switch dev


git switch -c <branch>
Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b feature/model


git merge <branch>
Merge a branch into the current one
git merge feature/clean_data


git rebase <branch>
Rebase current branch onto another (rewrites history)
git rebase dev


git log
Show commit history
git log --oneline --graph


git log --oneline
Compact history view
git log --oneline


git log --graph
Visualize branch structure
git log --graph




Tip: git switch and git restore are newer commands that separate concerns from the overloaded git checkout. Use them for clarity.

Remote Repositories – clone vs fork

Clone – creates a local copy of a remote repository.

git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

Fork – GitHub‑only: copy a repo to your own account.

Use to contribute without write access.

Exercise: Clone and Push
Goal: Clone a repository, make a change, and push.
# Clone the team repository
git clone https://github.com/ndmc-davt/zero-dose-project.git
cd zero-dose-project

# Make a change (e.g., add a data source note)
echo " - Data source: DHS 2020" >> README.md
git add README.md
git commit -m "Add data source note"
git push origin main

[Done] You have pushed a change to a remote repository.

Collaboration – Pull and Push

git pull = fetch + merge
git push uploads local commits


Always pull before push to avoid conflicts.

Exercise: Simulate Collaboration
Pair up: One person creates a repo, the other forks/clones.

Owner creates repo on GitHub, adds Collaborator.
Collaborator clones, makes change, pushes.
Owner pulls and merges.
Switch roles.


This is the core of team Git workflow.

Handling Merge Conflicts

Conflict: same file, same lines, different changes.
Git marks conflict with <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>.

<<<<<<< HEAD
method = "mean"
=======
method = "median"
>>>>>>> branch-name
Resolve: edit file, remove markers, then:
git add <file>
git commit -m "Resolved conflict"

section: Exercise: Create and Resolve a Conflict
text:
Exercise: Create and Resolve a Conflict
Setup: Two people (or two branches) modify the same line in a script.

Branch A: changes line 1 to method = "mean".
Branch B: changes line 1 to method = "median".
Merge A into dev, then try to merge B – conflict occurs.
Resolve by picking one method or combining.


[Done] You have resolved a real conflict.

Advanced: git rebase (vs merge)

rebase rewrites commit history to appear linear.
Use only on local branches before pushing.

git switch feature
git rebase dev    # move feature commits on top of dev

Then fast-forward merge: git switch dev; git merge feature


Caution: Do not rebase shared branches – use merge for public history.

Tags – Marking Releases

Lightweight vs annotated tags.
git tag v1.0 (lightweight)
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Release version 1.0"
Push tags: git push origin --tags

# Create annotated tag after completing a milestone (e.g., data ingestion done)
git tag -a v0.1 -m "Data ingestion pipeline complete"
git push origin v0.1

Tags are great for marking milestones (e.g., submission deadline).

.gitignore – Ignoring Unwanted Files
Prevent committing temporary, large, or sensitive files.
# Python
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.venv/

# R
.Rproj.user
.Rhistory
.RData
renv/library/

# Jupyter
.ipynb_checkpoints/

# Data
*.csv
*.nc
*.h5

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
.DS_Store

Add .gitignore early and commit it.

Large File Management – Git LFS
The Problem: Git stores every version of every file. Large files (datasets, models, images) bloat the repository, make clones slow, and can exceed GitHub’s 100 MB file limit.
The Solution: Git Large File Storage (LFS) replaces large files with small text pointers, storing the actual content on a separate server.
Installing Git LFS:
# Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
sudo apt install git-lfs

# macOS
brew install git-lfs

# Windows
# Download from https://git-lfs.com
Initialise LFS in your repository:
git lfs install
Track large file types:
# Track all CSV, NetCDF, and HDF5 files
git lfs track "*.csv"
git lfs track "*.nc"
git lfs track "*.h5"

# Track model files
git lfs track "*.pkl"
git lfs track "*.joblib"

# Track large images
git lfs track "*.tif"
git lfs track "*.png"
Commit the tracking configuration:
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "Track large files with Git LFS"
Push as usual:
git push origin main

Note: GitHub has a free LFS quota (1 GB storage, 1 GB bandwidth per month). For larger datasets, consider DVC.

DVC – Data Version Control (Advanced)

DVC is a tool that extends Git to handle large datasets and model files.
It stores data on cloud storage (S3, GCS, Azure) or local drives, while Git tracks small metadata files.

Install DVC:
pip install dvc
Initialise DVC:
dvc init
Track data:
dvc add data/raw/dataset.csv
git add data/raw/dataset.csv.dvc
git commit -m "Add dataset with DVC"
Push data to remote storage:
dvc remote add -d myremote s3://my-bucket/data
dvc push

When to use: Git LFS for files up to a few GB, DVC for very large datasets (10+ GB) or when you need versioned pipelines.

Exercise: Large File Management
Goal: Set up Git LFS and track a large file.
# 1. Install Git LFS (if not already)
git lfs install

# 2. Create a dummy large file (e.g., 200 MB) simulating a climate dataset
dd if=/dev/zero of=data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc bs=1M count=200

# 3. Track NetCDF files with LFS
git lfs track "*.nc"

# 4. Stage and commit
git add .gitattributes data/raw/raw_climate_data.nc
git commit -m "Add large climate dataset with LFS"

# 5. Push (LFS files will be uploaded separately)
git push origin main

[Done] You have successfully added a large file using Git LFS.

GitHub Issues – Tracking Tasks

Use Issues to track bugs, features, or tasks.
Each issue has a number, labels, milestones, and assignees.
Link commits with #issue-number to automatically close.

git commit -m "Fix bug #42"
git push
# When merged, issue #42 auto-closes.

Issues are essential for team coordination.

GitHub Projects – Kanban Board

Create a Project board (e.g., “Hackathon Tasks”).
Add columns: To Do, In Progress, Done.
Link issues to cards.
Visualise progress at a glance.


Great for managing hackathon workflow.

GitHub Actions – CI/CD

Automate testing, linting, deployment.
Define workflows in .github/workflows/*.yml.

Example Python test workflow:
name: CI
on: [push]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest

Set up Actions to ensure code quality automatically.

Exercise: GitHub Issues and Projects
Goal: Create an issue and a project board.

On your GitHub repo, go to Issues → New issue.
Create an issue: “Add data validation function” (or similar for your challenge).
Go to Projects → New project (Kanban).
Add the issue to the “To Do” column.
Assign yourself and a milestone (optional).


This is how you manage tasks collaboratively.

section: Environment Management – Using uv (Python) and renv (R)
text:
Environment Management – Using uv (Python) and renv (R)
After installing uv (earlier), here is how to use it in your project.

Python (uv)R (renv)


Initialise a project:
cd your_project
uv init
uv venv
source .venv/bin/activate   # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
Add dependencies:
uv add pandas numpy scikit-learn
uv lock                # creates uv.lock – commit this!
When a teammate clones:
uv sync                # installs exact versions from uv.lock

Commit pyproject.toml and uv.lock to Git.



Install renv (once per machine):
install.packages("renv")
Initialise a project:
renv::init()   # creates renv.lock and renv/activate.R
Add a package:
renv::install("dplyr")
renv::snapshot()   # updates renv.lock
When a teammate clones:
renv::restore()    # installs packages from renv.lock

Commit renv.lock and renv/.gitignore to Git.

Project Structure – CCDS (Cross‑Language)
A clean, reproducible project structure works for both Python and R. The AP R template generates a slightly different structure (with analysis/, etl/, publish/, viz/), but it is equally valid. If you prefer the standard CCDS layout, use the manual alternative.
project-name/
├── checkpoints/          # Model checkpoints (if applicable)
├── data/
│   ├── raw/              # Original, immutable data
│   └── processed/        # Cleaned, model‑ready data
├── docs/                 # Project documentation
├── notebooks/            # Jupyter (Python) or R Markdown/Quarto
├── src/                  # Reusable functions (Python) or R/
├── scripts/              # Analysis pipelines
├── tests/                # Unit tests
├── models/               # Saved model artifacts
├── reports/              # Generated reports, figures
├── pyproject.toml        # Python (uv)
├── requirements.txt      # Python fallback
├── renv.lock             # R (renv)
├── README.md
└── .gitignore

Team Challenges – Final Project (Four Groups)
Divide into four teams. Each team tackles one challenge using the main/dev/feature branch model.



Family
Challenge




Alpha
Zero‑dose Burden Mapping


Beta
Antimalarial Medicine Forecasting


Gamma
Measles Alert Rule Optimisation


Delta
Measles Transmission Modelling



Instructions with branch strategy:

One member creates a GitHub repo and adds all team members as collaborators.
Everyone clones the repo.
Create the dev branch from main:
git checkout -b dev
git push -u origin dev
Each member creates a feature branch from dev (e.g., feature/data-load, feature/clean, feature/model, feature/viz).
git switch dev
git pull origin dev
git checkout -b feature/your-task
Work in parallel – write code, commit, push to your feature branch.
Open Pull Requests (or merge locally) to merge each feature into dev.
Resolve any conflicts that arise during merges.
When all features are integrated into dev and tested, merge dev into main.
Final repo must include:

Project structure (CCDS or AP template).
Environment lock file (uv.lock or renv.lock).
.gitignore.
README.md.
Issues/Project board.
Large files managed with LFS/DVC.



This workflow mirrors a real‑world collaborative data science project.

text:
Git Command Cheat Sheet (1/2) – Local & Basic Commands








Command
Description
Example




git init
Initialise a new repository
git init


git status
Show working directory and staging status
git status


git add <file>
Stage changes for commit

git add .
Stage all changes in current directory
git add .


git commit -m "msg"
Commit staged changes

git log

git diff

Show staged changes
git diff --staged

section: Git Command Cheat Sheet (2/2) – Remote, Branching, & Advanced
text:
Git Command Cheat Sheet (2/2) – Remote, Branching, & Advanced








Command
Description
Example




git clone <url>
Copy a remote repository locally
git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git


git branch
List branches
git branch


git branch <name>
Create a new branch
git branch feature/clean


git switch <branch>
Switch to an existing branch
git switch dev


git checkout -b <branch>
Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b feature/model


git merge <branch>
Merge a branch into current
git merge feature/clean


git rebase <branch>
Rebase current branch onto another
git rebase dev


git pull
Fetch and merge remote changes
git pull origin dev


git push
Upload local commits to remote
git push origin dev


git push -u origin <branch>
Push and set upstream
git push -u origin feature/model


git remote -v
List remote repositories
git remote -v


git remote add <name> <url>
Add a new remote
git remote add origin git@github.com:user/repo.git


git fetch
Download remote changes without merging
git fetch origin


git tag
List tags
git tag


git tag -a <name> -m "msg"
Create an annotated tag
git tag -a v1.0 -m "Release v1.0"


git push origin --tags
Push tags to remote
git push origin --tags




Use git help <command> or git <command> --help for more details.

Git Tips and Tricks

Commit early, commit often – small, frequent commits are easier to review and revert if needed.
Write meaningful commit messages – use imperative mood, keep under 50 characters, explain why not how.
Run git status before and after every command – it prevents surprises and keeps you oriented.
Always git pull before you git push – this avoids unnecessary merge conflicts.
Use branches for every new feature or experiment – keep main stable; branch names like feature/description help.
Stash unfinished work – use git stash push -m "WIP" to save work in progress, then git stash pop to restore.
Review changes with git diff before staging – catch mistakes before they become commits.
Use git log --oneline --graph for a clear history view – visualise branches and merges at a glance.
Add .gitignore at the start – prevent accidental commits of data files, credentials, or cache folders.
Use Git LFS or DVC for large files – avoid repository bloat and stay within GitHub’s size limits.
If in doubt, git help or git <command> --help – the built‑in documentation is excellent.

section: Using Git from RStudio and VS Code
text:
Using Git from RStudio and VS Code
RStudio

Create a new project from an existing directory or clone a repository.
Use the Git pane to stage, commit, push, and pull.
View history and diff changes with a GUI.

VS Code

The built-in Source Control panel shows changed files.
Stage changes, commit, push/pull with one click.
Extensions like GitLens add powerful history visualisation.


Both tools work seamlessly with the same Git commands.

section: Summary – The R & Python Hackathon Toolkit
text:
Summary – The R & Python Hackathon Toolkit

Git – tracks code, enables branching, works offline.
GitHub – collaboration platform with Issues, Projects, Actions.
Git LFS / DVC – manage large datasets and models.
Environment managers – uv (Python) and renv (R) for reproducibility.
Project templates – CCDS (Python) and Associated Press R template (R).
Branch strategy – main for production, dev for integration, feature/* for new work.


Remember: Always pull before you push, write clear commit messages, and use issues to track progress.
Thank You & Happy Hacking!

Happy Hacking!
